title: Product Costing
description: Quiz covering product costing methods, costing systems and their role in managerial decision-making.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of product costing?
options: To determine the total cost of producing a product or service
questions.explanation: Product costing determines all costs associated with producing a product or delivering a service.

questions.question: Which three cost categories are typically included in a product's total manufacturing cost?
options: Direct materials, direct labor and manufacturing overhead
Selling expenses, marketing expenses and dividends
questions.explanation: A product's manufacturing cost normally consists of direct materials, direct labor and allocated manufacturing overhead.

questions.question: Why is accurate product costing important?
options: It helps managers make better pricing and profitability decisions
questions.explanation: Managers rely on accurate product costs when pricing products and evaluating profitability.

questions.question: Which costing method is most appropriate for building custom yachts?
questions.explanation: Custom-built products are unique projects, making Job Costing the appropriate method.

questions.question: Which costing method is commonly used in a factory producing identical bottles of mineral water?
questions.explanation: Process Costing is used for continuous production of identical products.

questions.question: What characterizes Job Costing?
options: Each job or project is costed separately
Every product receives exactly the same cost
questions.explanation: Job Costing tracks costs individually for each customer order or project.

questions.question: Which industry is most likely to use Process Costing?
questions.explanation: Oil refineries continuously produce standardized products, making Process Costing appropriate.

questions.question: What does Full Costing include?
options: All manufacturing costs, including both direct and indirect costs
questions.explanation: Full Costing assigns every manufacturing cost to the product.

questions.question: What is the main goal of Activity-Based Costing (ABC)?
options: To allocate overhead more accurately based on activities
questions.explanation: ABC improves costing accuracy by assigning overhead based on the activities that consume resources.

questions.question: A company discovers that its actual manufacturing cost per product is much higher than previously estimated. What is the most likely consequence?
options: The company may need to increase its selling price or reduce costs
The product becomes a direct cost
The company no longer needs product costing
questions.explanation: Higher production costs may require pricing adjustments or cost reductions to remain profitable.

questions.question: A construction company builds a new office building for a customer. Which costing method is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Construction projects are unique, making Job Costing the appropriate method.

questions.question: Why is Process Costing generally unsuitable for custom-built products?
options: Because each product has unique costs that should be tracked separately
Because Process Costing ignores labor costs
Because it only includes indirect costs
questions.explanation: Custom products require individual cost tracking rather than averaging costs across identical units.

questions.question: A factory produces 40,000 identical chocolate bars each day. Which costing method would normally provide the most efficient cost calculation?
questions.explanation: Large-scale production of identical products is well suited for Process Costing.

questions.question: Which statement best describes Full Costing?
options: Every manufactured product receives both direct costs and a share of manufacturing overhead
Only direct materials are assigned to products
Only indirect costs are assigned to products
questions.explanation: Full Costing includes all manufacturing costs when calculating product cost.

questions.question: What is a cost driver in Activity-Based Costing (ABC)?
options: A factor that causes an activity to consume resources
The selling price of a product
questions.explanation: Cost drivers explain why activities generate costs and are used to allocate overhead more accurately.

questions.question: A company manufactures two products using the same labor hours. However, Product A requires many more machine setups than Product B. Which costing method is most likely to reflect this difference?
options: Traditional Full Costing using only labor hours
Job Costing based only on direct labor
questions.explanation: ABC allocates setup costs to the product that actually requires more setup activities.

questions.question: Why can traditional costing sometimes produce misleading product costs?
options: Because it may use only one allocation base for all overhead costs
Because it only works for service companies
questions.explanation: Using a single allocation base may not reflect how different products consume resources.

questions.question: A company introduces Activity-Based Costing and discovers that a low-volume product is much more expensive to produce than previously believed. What is the most likely reason?
options: The product consumes more support activities than expected
Revenue was included as a manufacturing cost
questions.explanation: Low-volume products often require proportionally more setups, inspections or engineering activities.

questions.question: Which costing method is generally the simplest to implement?
options: Neither—they require the same level of detail
Job Costing with multiple cost drivers
questions.explanation: Traditional costing is simpler because it usually relies on fewer allocation bases than ABC.

questions.question: A manufacturing company produces many different products with varying complexity. Which costing approach is likely to provide the most accurate product costs?
options: Process Costing using one average cost
questions.explanation: ABC captures differences in resource consumption between complex and simple products.

questions.question: A company discovers that one of its products is much less profitable after implementing Activity-Based Costing (ABC). What is the best first response?
options: Investigate why the product consumes so many activities before making decisions
questions.explanation: Managers should first understand the reasons behind the higher costs before changing pricing or production.

questions.question: Why might two products with identical direct materials and direct labor have different total product costs?
options: Because they consume different amounts of manufacturing overhead
Because product costs never include overhead
questions.explanation: Products often require different levels of support activities, resulting in different overhead allocations.

questions.question: A consulting firm completes projects that vary greatly in size and complexity. Which costing method is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Each consulting project is unique and should have its own accumulated costs.

questions.question: A paper mill produces thousands of identical rolls of paper every day. Why is Process Costing the preferred method?
options: Because production is continuous and the units are nearly identical
questions.explanation: Process Costing averages costs across a large number of identical units.

questions.question: A manager prices a product using only direct materials and direct labor while ignoring manufacturing overhead. What is the greatest risk?
options: The selling price may be too low to cover the product's true cost
The product automatically becomes more profitable
The company will eliminate fixed costs
Revenue will increase regardless of costs
questions.explanation: Ignoring overhead can significantly underestimate the true production cost.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why Activity-Based Costing (ABC) often provides more accurate product costs than traditional costing?
options: It allocates overhead using multiple cost drivers that better reflect resource consumption
It assumes every product uses resources equally
questions.explanation: ABC recognizes that different activities generate different costs and assigns them accordingly.

questions.question: A factory introduces a highly automated production line. Which allocation base may become more appropriate than direct labor hours for assigning overhead?
questions.explanation: In automated production, machine usage often drives overhead costs more than labor hours.

questions.question: Why do many companies continue using traditional costing even when ABC may be more accurate?
options: Because traditional costing is simpler and less expensive to implement
Because traditional costing is always more accurate
questions.explanation: ABC requires more detailed data collection and is often more costly to maintain.

questions.question: A manufacturing company introduces a new costing system and discovers that several products previously considered profitable are actually generating losses. What does this demonstrate?
options: Accurate costing can significantly improve managerial decision-making
Product costing has no impact on profitability
Direct costs should always be ignored
questions.explanation: Better cost information helps managers make more informed decisions about pricing, production and product portfolios.

questions.question: What is the ultimate purpose of product costing?
options: To provide reliable cost information that supports pricing, profitability analysis and strategic decision-making
To maximize revenue regardless of costs
To simplify bookkeeping by removing indirect costs
questions.explanation: Product costing exists to give managers accurate information for making sound business decisions.
